title: Quo vadis, stochastic thermodynamics?
authors: Jan Korbel, Artemy Kolchinsky, Sarah A. M. Loos, Gonzalo Manzano, Rosalba Garcia-Millan, Olga Movilla Miangolarra, Édgar Roldán
abstract: Stochastic thermodynamics is a framework for describing non-equilibrium processes at the level of fluctuating trajectories, where the state of a system evolves as a stochastic time series, allowing thermodynamic quantities such as work, heat, and entropy production to be defined along individual realizations rather than at the ensemble level only. Over the past three decades, the field has yielded fundamental results, including fluctuation theorems and several universal bounds, such as thermodynamic uncertainty relations, speed limit theorems, and many others. Many of them have been tested on a range of experimental platforms. This Perspective reviews recent developments in stochastic thermodynamics that extend its scope beyond its traditional domains, including systems with memory and hidden degrees of freedom, microscopic approaches to interacting and active matter, and geometric formulations based on optimal transport. Next, the Perspective surveys the challenges that arise when applying these ideas to macroscopic and complex systems, where the link between statistical irreversibility and thermodynamic dissipation becomes less direct. Finally, emerging applications in non-physical contexts are highlighted, including computation, biological systems, and social dynamics. Transcending the traditional boundaries of physics, these developments catalyze an unorthodox framework to tackle the thermodynamics of complex systems.
summary: This Perspective reviews the evolution of stochastic thermodynamics from a theory of small-scale fluctuations to a broader framework for complex systems. It covers recent progress in handling memory effects, hidden variables, and interacting active matter. Crucially, it addresses the 'decoupling' problem where statistical irreversibility no longer directly quantifies energy dissipation in macroscopic systems. Finally, it explores the application of these thermodynamic principles to biological, computational, and social dynamics.
main_problem: The paper explores the expansion of stochastic thermodynamics from its traditional focus on microscopic fluctuations to complex, large-scale, and non-physical systems, specifically addressing the decoupling of statistical irreversibility from physical energy dissipation at macroscopic scales.
main_result: The authors review how the framework has evolved to include memory effects, hidden degrees of freedom, and active matter, while highlighting the challenge that thermodynamic bounds become increasingly loose and disconnected from energy dissipation in macroscopic/social contexts.
method: The review synthesizes various theoretical frameworks including Langevin/Fokker-Planck dynamics, Doi-Peliti field theory, optimal transport (Wasserstein distance), and information-theoretic approaches.
model_or_system: The scope covers a wide range of systems including molecular motors, active matter (run-and-tumble particles), biological networks (cytoskeleton, brain dynamics), and non-physical systems like computational and social dynamics.
key_observables: Entropy production (housekeeping and excess), work, heat, dissipation, particle currents, and time-reversal asymmetry.
important_parameters: Microscopic vs. macroscopic scales, Markovian vs. non-Markovian regimes, and the signal-to-noise ratio in currents.
main_assumptions: The paper notes that the link between statistical irreversibility and thermodynamic dissipation is less direct in coarse-grained or macroscopic systems, and that embedding hidden variables can lead to non-unique interpretations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides an illustration of a nano-sized motor in biological tissue using fuel and feedback to perform work while dissipating heat.
paper_structure: The paper follows a historical and thematic progression: starting with the foundations of fluctuation theorems, moving to recent extensions (memory, hidden variables, active matter), discussing geometric formulations (optimal transport), addressing the challenges of scaling to macroscopic/complex systems, and concluding with applications in non-physical domains.
why_interesting: It is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it discusses the fundamental limits of dissipation, the role of memory/non-Markovianity, and the geometric structure of non-equilibrium processes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of the ‘dream’ of early developments of stochastic thermodynamics. A tiny nano-sized motor navigates through a biological tissue, tracing a stochastic trajectory. Such motion, powered by fuel and controlled through feedback upon information retrieval, is converted partially into useful work while the rest is dissipated to the surrounding media as heat. The first decades of stochastic thermodynamics research aimed to understand the statistical constraints for various dynamical and thermodynamical properties of such small-scale non-equilibrium processes. AI-generated image using Gemini, inspired by a presentation by Ignacio A. Martínez and on the movie Innerspace (1987).
